speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kaufman, we left at the last tape in the year of 1940, when you were in the Cracow Ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a typical day in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very dull day. We got up in the morning. We're trying to find some--

speaker: interviewer
text: What time would you wake up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early, late, there was nothing to rush for. There was nothing to do. We had no jobs, no-- we had nothing. But it was just trying to find to eat. Clothes we had very little because we went to a-- we had-- we had to leave everything when we left. And I went to work because I went-- I was picked up in buses, like, with my girlfriend who lived in same apartment. She helped me to get that job at the airport. And we were working. In the evening, we were coming back. And my parents were there with my brothers. I think my brothers were working in a-- there was a-- they were calling that Gemeinschaft. It was clothing-making or whatever. I really didn't know too much about because I was busy on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you selected to work at the airport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through my girlfriend I got the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it preferable to work than not to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, much better. I wasn't hungry because there were-- the Air, Air Force was feeding us. And the job wasn't bad because we just had to clean the offices, the girls. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to trade anything while you were outside of the ghetto for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have what to trade. That was the problem because we had to live the way we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your brother had a child in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you elaborate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I tell you honestly, in Poland, a, a girl like I was, I didn't know too much about the whole thing. But I knew they were-- he had a baby. The baby was born and midwife delivered. And after when was the birth, eight days later, the rabbi came and he said, it has to be named Shalom for peace. So she named it. And the baby got typhoid. They took him to a hospital. And came the German woman, [SOBBING] killed it. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The baby was already three, four months old. [SOBBING] And we knew about.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you living in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't too long in the ghetto because I was at the airport. And after that, we went to the-- Plaszow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in Plaszow, I was going out to work. I was always lucky that I got a job-- I always working. Like with Schindler, he had, at the time, not ammunition factory, but he had a pots-and-pans factory. So I working there. And I really wasn't working in pots and pans because they started already with ammunition.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was lucky enough. I was very good-looking at the time. So I got boyfriends. One Juden helped me. The other one helped me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were-- I was working on the ammunition at Schindler's. He was taking us back to Plaszow. No, I'm sorry. I think he had a residence for us made over there where we were working. So I was separated with my parents, but I could see them after when I was going-- coming to the ghetto. But it was tough in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were still in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents were in the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: After, from the ghetto, they were taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what year you went to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '41--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: --end of '41, maybe?

speaker: interviewer
text: --less than a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, less, less than a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to Plaszow, your parents were still in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went to Plaszow, no, my, my parents never went to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: --were still in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were giving out, like, blue cards, green cards. And there were a segregation of the Jewish people. So with the blue cards, you were going to Plaszow. With the green cards, you were going out, out of town.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- my parents were hiding already in the ghetto because my sister-in-law's parents had a business there. So they had the keys. And they were hiding. And I was with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I said to my mother, if you all stay here, we were [INAUDIBLE]. One has to go out to look for work. So I went out and looking-- and went-- when I went out, a German caught me and pushed me away. I didn't know where I'm going. But when he pushed me, I found myself in-- I ran after the people who were going-- at the airport. So I went with them on the bus. I was lucky. That's all it really was. I went back in the evening-- that they told me-- my mother heard that the German took me someplace. And she was sure that he shot me. So my mother and father, my sister-in-law, [SOBBING] and the two brothers went [CRYING] to the-- [SOBBING] to the group of people who were going already. I don't know where. I don't know where.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in the ghetto were they able to hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: In buildings, just like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Under furniture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had furniture in the ghetto. But it was-- at the time when they started to pick up the Jews, nothing was anymore-- we didn't need nothing. We just had to hide that they wouldn't find you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my parents were lucky because-- knew for-- that my sister-in-law's parents had that factory over there, the same where the ghetto was. And they went to hide over there. So it was sleeping on floors or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went out. And there, I didn't know how I survive-- found myself completely different. But somebody reported to my mother-- that I found out later that they caught me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So mother my mother and father said, what are we waiting for? Let's go out. We will choke anyway here. And we would have what to eat. So they, they didn't realize what's going on. And they reported to the transport. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got a little card from my father, who-- he must have dropped from the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're going. We don't know where-- and it-- some other-- some other words-- a little card. And I haven't got it because after that, I had throw away-- everything away.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the ghetto, did you ever hear of the term 'concentration camp'?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We knew only that whoever went with the transport never came back. But what's going on-- nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you witness to many of your Jewish friends, acquaintances leaving on these transports?

speaker: interviewee
text: On this one? I don't know on which one. Or--

speaker: interviewer
text: In general, while you were at the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, [SIGHS] ghetto was awful. They make it-- they made it right away smaller because there was some inspection coming on them, too. So the ghetto was smaller when we were coming back from work. But when I came back from work, there was nobody there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any conversations you've ever had with your family or your friends about the future of the Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all sure that we're going to die. There was no future. There was not something to look forward that, OK, tomorrow will be better. Or everybody expect, OK, this one went today. Till-- tomorrow, somebody else will go. We were prepared for it. I just survived because luck. I wasn't smarter than somebody else. I admit that-- just lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the ghetto, had you ever heard of the name Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I heard in Plaszow. And I found myself in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: One last question about the Cracow Ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I say 'Cracow Ghetto,' what is the strongest image you have in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be scared what tonight, tomorrow, or, or this minute, maybe they're coming-- hear some noises. That was the living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for us the first day in the Plaszow labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll be honest with you. I didn't been too long there in Plaszow because I was, again, lucky enough that I was chosen to go to Schindler to do work in the-- with the pots and pans over there. So I lived over there. And I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the question, really? What is in Plaszow, or-- What was Plaszow like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Plaszow was [SIGHS] just like a hell. That was right-- a mountain-- they're up in a mountain and killing every day more people, more people. People were going to work, but majority-- every day you could hear it. Like my own husband, they took them, and-- for no reason, they took him from, from the barrack where he was sleeping and put him in a hall where the same day, they killed 200, 300 people and-- naked already in the hall to be shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody said to the German, is-- there be a inspection? Those people have to go out because we're having-- they'll find out that we killing everybody. So-- went out. And my husband was between them, got dressed, came back to Plaszow-- just lucky. Did you meet your husband in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at Schindler in Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I knew him before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Plaszow before you were put on Schindler's List?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working for Schindler, like I said, with the pots and pans. How long I was in Plaszow? Not too long, maybe six months, five months. I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the entire time you were in Plaszow, you were working for Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how you got on the list?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, lucky, just lucky-- just luck. Lucky-- I remember when I went to Brunnlitz with Schindler. It was again an Appell. I'm sure by now you know what means 'Appell,' when they put together the Jews. And they started to count and the-- then came out names.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had my girlfriend standing beside me, who lives now in, in Montreal. And she said to me, Esther, you, you know the guy who made the list? I knew him, a Jewish fellow who was helping make the list for Schindler. Do you recall his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goldberg. And he was-- he wasn't too nice to the Jewish people after. Some of them-- to me, he was nice. And suddenly, they called my name. And I went to Plaszow-- to Brunnlitz. Or why and what-- people, whatever they had, you know, on their body, they were giving away to that Goldberg to be on that list because we heard that Schindler maybe will save you. We really didn't know exactly who he is. And I was called to go. And I went. My parents were not there anymore. I didn't know where my brothers-- I was just alone. But I had a nature, you know, I wouldn't go-- I go a straight line. I wouldn't go-- this-- my destiny takes me here. Maybe it's good. And that, I believed in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if Oskar Schindler ever had opportunity to see you before you were recruited?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was working in-- with pots and pans, he was coming every day. He was coming. He was checking. And after we were starting on ammunition-- and he had inspection on him. He wasn't-- it wasn't so easy for him, either, because the were-- they were-- the Gestapo coming and checking if he's doing his job. But he was a nice guy. He was very clever-- very clever.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were working for the pots-and-pans manufacturing factory, were you still living at Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, yeah. And after that, he made some living quarters for us. And I had-- I don't know how many people could have been there, 500 maybe-- young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: And following that did you go back to Plaszow yes because from Plaszow, I went to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you living at the pots-and-pans factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall how long you were in Plaszow between the pots-and-pans factory and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: --ammunitions factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long. Again, I was very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: How so?

speaker: interviewee
text: A month or something. I really don't remember exactly. I don't want to put dates or what because I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Untersturmfuhrer of Plaszow was Amon 'Go-oth.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Goeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Goathe.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Goeth. I have his picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have opportunity to see him, to meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [SIGHS] if I would meet him, I wouldn't be talking to you. Whoever met him, that was it. Very-- no, I didn't. I was a lucky one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a witness to any of the murders that he had committed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. If he was-- if he had to pass by us-- as a matter of fact, he hanged two of my good friends, two boys who had started to run away from Plaszow. And they were caught. Up And we had to pass by to see how they're hanging.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Oneg Fuchs. And the other one, I don't remember. But one I remember for sure-- beautiful two boys, just beautiful. And they're each about 17, 18-- gorgeous boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the labor camp, did you ever have opportunity to celebrate any religious holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to?

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's-- I don't know we knew. We knew about Yom Kippur. We were fasting. I don't know [LAUGHS] with what-- we had enough to fast. But we-- but we knew. I don't know how that we find out. That, I don't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after Plaszow was liquidated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Brunnlitz with Schindler-- Schindler's List. We all went-- not to Brunnlitz, to Auschwitz. And in Auschwitz, we were about three and a half weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you part of the selection process at Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I went-- Schindler took me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had you been working for Schindler up to that point, do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't-- I cannot tell you exactly how long. I was working from the Plaszow and from Emalia. But that had nothing to do because that was a list what they made for him, who is going, who is not going. And I was lucky enough that I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you were on the list--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before your name was called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I told you before, I didn't. My girlfriend said, do you know-- Marcel Goldberg was his name. Ask him to put-- that's-- I heard it's very good, which she didn't know what she was talking. And I said, you know, let me-- let me rest. I don't know. I don't push myself. And when I finished-- like I said, I don't like to repeat myself. So that-- called my name, and I went to, to Brunnlitz-- to Auschwitz. In Auschwitz, we were hoping that the promises will be there that he would take us out. And he did-- after three and a half weeks, he did. And nobody from us got killed. And there were elderly womens. There were-- one child, as a matter of fact, we were hiding, too, a little girl, what she went with us. And she survived. And we went to Brunnlitz. And he had a factory for us over there. We were working. We were working, but nothing was going out. You know, he used to say to us-- I have to put a nice word for him. Tomorrow, the inspection is coming, so you better watch it. Because if they catch you not, not-- doing nothing, I have problems. And he was-- if he could throw a birthday party for his wife, I wasn't at the birthday party. But he threw a piece of bread for us, more than usually. And sometimes, he shared three times a year the birthday party. He was very good to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If not Schindler, I wouldn't be talking to you, to Vancouver, that's for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would just like to backtrack another moment. When your name-- who called your name--

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the older mans, or whatever, couples, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was standing in a lineup. You know, they usually put us together-- and counting. And then my name was called to go there. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say 'they' lined us all up, who are you referring to?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends, all the people who were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if we were all the prisoners, but the majority of the prisoners were kind of out. What we-- I really didn't know-- I knew maybe I would save myself. But when I-- we came to Auschwitz in the middle of the night and German Shepherds waiting with lights on you, you think the world is coming to end. So no Schindler saved you. Nobody saved you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any conversations you had with any of the women?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're going to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew you were going to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew, we realized that we are going to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you realize--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we see trains. This was already 1944-- November, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you not told that you were going to Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but we have to pass Auschwitz. But who believed anything? So we-- the train was going. We didn't know where it's going. But when it stopped, the train-- middle of the night, darkness, and just the the lights were shining on you from every corner. No bathroom on the train for three four days, nothing to eat. And we landed. German shepherds were all surrounded. Nobody could-- there was no question of running away. So he-- and we were designated to all kinds of barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard the term 'Auschwitz,' the name 'Auschwitz'?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew already. We knew. We knew about concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that you're going to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know this? Just it was spread. People maybe came back with something. Everybody knew already. 1944, there was-- whoever's away, and lots of people died already. That was just the end of-- like of the city Cracow. And it was a big-- I think 60,000 Jews or something that was in in Cracow. Can you describe the events that followed after they opened the doors to the train at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, the German Shepherd would greet you. And the lights in your face and-- pitch dark at night. And after they line us up, in the designated barracks for was, the barracks was just wood and rats from every corner.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, like myself, I'm scared of mouse, and I'm scared of rats. So I, I don't know from where I got the stick. So for as long I was in Auschwitz, I was sleeping like that. Everybody knew me. I was doing that, so nothing would touch me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were not working. Just one day they were caught-- some people who had to give blood for the German Army. I was lucky enough, again, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen had you given blood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will give blood because they were just taking blood as long-- as soon-- she fainted or whatever, he fainted. They stopped. No, but woman-- but they were-- I'm sorry, it was only women. Auschwitz was all women. My husband went to Gross Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall how many women were with you who were all promised to go--

speaker: interviewee
text: To, to Brunnlitz? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 350? Could it be possible? I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any-- were any of them requested to give blood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, and majority give blood-- a majority. Very few-- I had to clean the barracks. So I didn't go. And I-- they didn't know where they're going, where they are taking. So when they came back-- so then you were lucky. You, you didn't give blood. We all give blood. So-- but the blood was good for them. But the Jews were no good. The Jewish blood was good. They needed blood for the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out you were leaving Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us make us ready-- ready with whatever we had. And we had line-ups. And we were taking-- how did we go to-- we-- you were asking me about leaving Auschwitz, yes? Again by train, I think, but we were going-- we knew where we going, that Schindler is taking us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I really do not know. But we knew that maybe we would be safe here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall how--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- excuse me-- that was exactly November the 10th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. And we were liberated in Brunnlitz, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall--

speaker: interviewee
text: April.

speaker: interviewer
text: --any conversations you had on the transport from Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you felt when the doors opened, and you were, in fact, in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just happy. We didn't know how long the happiness is going to last. But happy that least no German Shepherd and no sticks, and nobody is beating us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you greeted by anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I-- [INAUDIBLE]. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given any specific task after you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, was the factory there. We had to work in the factory. But we knew that nothing is going-- know, looked like we are very busy. And we were not busy. We were doing nothing, just sitting at that chair and doing. Like, my machine was three feet taller than I was. And I, I couldn't reach to the whole thing. But Schindler knew when they come to check on him. He was very clever. And he goes, you sit and show that you work there, do something. Well, nothing came out from there, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which factory was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition with Schindler. He was making ammunition for them supposedly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what his plans were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hoping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hoping for what?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- for freedom, that that's-- we, we had the feeling that the end is coming. I don't know why. But we had the feeling that the end is coming. But from where, from-- I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we knew that Cracow was taken already-- January. I remember like now, January I knew German had to leave because Cracow was taken already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any personal communications with Mr. Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I worked for about a week in his kitchen. And the communication was-- he was a heavy smoker. And I can show you he smoked like that, holding the cigarette and dropping-- one puff and dropped the cigarette. And I picked it up. And there, I learned how to smoke. I never smoked in my life. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the only direct contact you had with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was talking to us. He-- you know, he was very friendly, very-- good guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say that you had to work when 'they' inspected it, who are you referring to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon. What was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that Schindler knew when 'they' were coming--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are 'they'?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was smart enough-- 'they'-- the Gestapo. He was smart enough, like, I know by fact that he was drinking. They, they put some Gestapos what he didn't like it. But he knew that they were going to report him. So he was drinking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the back of the-- they-- he report them. How can they watch the Jews if they, they are drinking? So they-- the inspection came, and they took them all away, the mean ones. And so he had to free them already to be with us. He, he really wanted to save us. Mrs. Kaufman, we're at the end of this tape. And we'll continue in a moment. Thank--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
